title: Naming Parts of a Shape: A Circle in Fourths

steps.instruction: A circle is divided into 4 parts. Are all parts the same size?
steps.work: Yes — the circle is split into 4 equal parts.
steps.explanation: To use fractions, the shape MUST be divided into equal parts. If parts are different sizes, we cannot name them with fractions.

steps.instruction: How many equal parts are there? This becomes the denominator.
steps.explanation: The denominator (bottom number) tells how many equal parts the whole is divided into.

steps.instruction: Each single part is 1 out of 4. Write the fraction.
steps.explanation: One part out of four equal parts is called one fourth, written as 1/4.

steps.instruction: If 3 parts are shaded, what fraction is shaded?
steps.explanation: The numerator counts how many parts are shaded. Three out of four equal parts = 3/4.

steps.instruction: A rectangle is divided into 3 parts. Two parts look small and one part looks big. Can we call each part 'one third'?
steps.work: The parts are NOT the same size.
steps.explanation: For a fraction name to apply, every part must be exactly the same size.

steps.work: 1/3 means one of three EQUAL parts. If parts are unequal, '1/3' does not apply.
steps.explanation: Fractions describe fair shares. If the parts are not equal, the fraction does not accurately describe the size of each piece.

steps.instruction: How could we fix this rectangle to show thirds?
steps.work: Divide the rectangle into 3 parts that are all the same size — for example, 3 equal columns.
steps.explanation: When each column is the same width, every part is exactly 1/3 of the rectangle.
